---
title: "Lesson3_Comments_and_Variables"
author: "Paul Lott"
date: "2024-05-13"
output:
  html_document:
    df_print: paged
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

# R Comments and Variables

## Comments

In R code, we can leave comments by adding `#`. Note it has the same
function in Python. Anything after the `#` is treated as a comment.
Therefore, if you want to skip running some line of code, you can
*comment* it and it won't be run in R.

```{r eval=FALSE}
example(vector)  # Get the Vector Examples

#example(list)  
```

## R Variables

### Variable Assignment

Variables in R are case sensitive and must start with a letter or `.`.
If you start a variable with a period, the second character must not be
a digit. There is no limit to variable name length.

In R documentation and tutorials, you'll see variables being defined
using `<-`. This denotes the direction of assignment

`x <- 10` \# Means that the value of 10 is being stored in x

```{r}
x<-10
print(x)
```

Alternatively, you can reverse the expression to make a right-handed
assignment. Note: This is different than Python, which requires
left-handed assignments only.

```{r}
9 -> x
print(x)
```

Finally, you'll also notice that R now recognizes `=` as an assignment
operator.

```{r}
x = 120
print(x)
```

It's important to remember, in most cases `=` is equivalent to `<-`.
However, if you ever use both `<-` and `=` in an expression, the `<-`
has precedence over `=` - meaning the `<-` will be evaluated first and
then `=` will be evaluated second.

```{r}
y <- 20
print(y)
```

Finally, `assign(name, value)` function is the equivalent of the `<-`
assignment operator. It provides additional additional options for
assignment. See `?assign` for more about the assign function.

```{r}
assign('x',256)
x
```

### Listing Variables

If you're not working in RStudio, you might not know what variables are
assigned in an environment. You can list the variables in your current
working session.

```{r}
ls()
```

An equivalent function to list the variables is `objects()`

```{r}
objects()
```

Both `ls()` and `object()` will return a vector (ie a type of list) of
variable names in your current session.

However, if you are working in RStudio the ***Environment*** pane (Upper
Right) displays the variables that are stored in your environment. So,
you can quickly look up and see at anytime what values are assigned and
which variables are defined. If you select the **Grid** option from the
Right side of the pane - you'll see some additional information

![](images/rstudio-panes-labeled.jpeg)

### Removing Variables

At some point you may want to remove a variable that you aren't going to
use anymore. This becomes vitally important, when you might have things
running in the same directory because anytime R gets shutdown - it'll
ask to save existing variables into a file `.RData`. When restarting the
session in this directory, even for a different script - it'll load the
variables that were previously stored from the `.RData` file.

To remove a variable:

```{r}
rm(y)
```

or

Select the variable **checkbox** and then use the broom in the
***Environment*** pane to delete the variable

------------------------------------------------------------------------
